lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does the while loop do?
option_b: Executes statements while a particular condition is true
option_c: Skips statements that match a condition
option_d: Executes statements only once if the condition is false
description: A while loop continually executes its body as long as its condition evaluates to true. As soon as the condition becomes false, the loop exits.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Which of these are essential elements of any loop?
option_b: Control variable, initial value, increment/decrement, continuation condition
description: Every loop needs four pieces: a control variable (counter), an initial value, an increment/decrement step, and a continuation condition that determines when to stop.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is printed by this code? int count = 0; while (count < 3) { System.out.print(count + " "); count++; }
description: Iteration prints 0, then 1, then 2. When count becomes 3 the condition count < 3 is false and the loop exits. Output: 0 1 2.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What happens if you forget to increment the loop counter inside a while loop?
description: Without an increment, the condition stays true forever and the loop never exits — this is an infinite loop. Java doesn't catch this at compile time.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is a sentinel (or flag) value?
option_b: A specific input value used to signal the end of a loop
option_c: A constant declared with the final keyword
option_d: A loop counter starting from zero
description: A sentinel value (a.k.a. flag value) is a special input chosen to tell the loop to stop — for example, 0 meaning "end of input" when summing user-entered numbers.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Why is it dangerous to use floating-point values for equality checking in a loop control?
option_a: Because floating-point math is faster than integer math
option_b: Because floating-point values are stored as approximations
option_c: Because Java doesn't allow doubles in loops
option_d: Because floats are slower than ints
description: Floating-point values are approximations in binary, so a comparison like x == 0.1 can be unexpectedly false. Always use a tolerance check (Math.abs(x - 0.1) < 1e-9) or use integers.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does the break statement do inside a loop?
option_a: Skips the current iteration and continues with the next
option_b: Restarts the loop from the beginning
option_c: Terminates the loop immediately and transfers control to the statement after it
option_d: Pauses the loop until user input is received
description: break terminates the loop entirely as soon as it's encountered, transferring control to the first statement after the loop.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does the continue statement do inside a loop?
option_b: Jumps to the beginning of the loop for the next iteration, skipping remaining statements in the current iteration
description: continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the loop's next iteration. Unlike break, the loop itself keeps running.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does this loop print? for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { if (i == 3) continue; System.out.print(i + " "); }
description: When i == 3, continue skips the print statement and goes to the next iteration. Output: 1 2 4 5.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does this loop print? for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { if (i == 3) break; System.out.print(i + " "); }
description: When i == 3, break exits the loop entirely before printing. Output: 1 2.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is the key difference between while and do-while?
option_b: do-while evaluates its condition at the bottom, so the body always executes at least once
option_c: do-while can only run a fixed number of times
description: In do-while the condition is checked after the body, guaranteeing the body runs at least once even if the condition is initially false. In while the condition is checked first and the body might not run at all.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is the syntax of a do-while loop?
description: Correct syntax: do { Statement(s); } while (condition); — note the semicolon at the end after the closing parenthesis.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is the syntax of a for loop?
description: Java's for syntax: for (initialization; termination; increment/decrement) { statement(s) }.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: When is the increment expression of a for loop executed?
option_c: After each iteration of the loop
description: The increment/decrement expression runs after every iteration of the loop body, just before the termination check is re-evaluated.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: When is the initialization expression of a for loop executed?
description: The initialization expression runs exactly once at the start of the loop. It's a great place to declare and initialize a loop counter.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Is this a valid for loop? for (;;) { System.out.println("hi"); }
option_a: No, all three parts are required
option_b: Yes, and it creates an infinite loop unless break is used
option_d: Yes, but it runs exactly once
description: All three parts are optional. for (;;) with no condition means always true, creating an infinite loop. You must use break (or return/throw) to exit.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What are nested loops?
option_a: Two loops that run in parallel threads
option_b: A loop placed inside another loop
option_c: Loops that cannot be exited with break
option_d: Loops that share the same counter variable
description: Nested loops are loops inside other loops. Each time the outer loop iterates, the inner loop runs its full cycle from the start.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does this nested loop print? for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { for (int j = 1; j <= 2; j++) { System.out.print(i + "" + j + " "); } }
description: Outer i goes 1, 2, 3. For each i, inner j goes 1, 2. So pairs are: 11, 12, 21, 22, 31, 32 → 11 12 21 22 31 32.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Which task is a classic example of nested loops?
description: A multiplication table is the classic nested-loop exercise: outer loop iterates rows (one factor), inner loop iterates columns (the other factor).

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is a method in Java?
option_c: A collection of statements grouped together to perform an operation
description: A method is a collection of statements grouped together to perform an operation. Methods enable reuse, hide implementation details, and reduce complexity.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Which of these is NOT a benefit of using methods?
option_d: Make the program run faster automatically
description: Methods don't automatically make programs faster — they're about structure: reuse, information hiding, and complexity reduction. Performance benefits, if any, are indirect.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: In the method declaration `public int max(int num1, int num2)`, what is `int` (the second word)?
description: Order in the header: modifier (public) → return type (int) → name (max) → parameters (int num1, int num2).

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Which access modifier makes a method visible only inside the class where it's defined?
description: private restricts visibility to the class only. public = visible to the world; protected = package + subclasses; void is a return type, not an access modifier.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Which access modifier makes a method visible to its package and all subclasses?
description: protected = visible to the same package and to all subclasses (even outside the package).

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What does it mean if a method's return type is `void`?
option_b: The method does not return any value
description: void means the method does not return any value. It's used for methods whose purpose is a side-effect (printing, modifying state) rather than producing a value.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: How are parameters passed to a Java method (per the lecture)?
description: Java parameters are passed by value — the method receives a copy of the value, so reassigning a parameter inside the method does NOT change the caller's variable.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: If a method returns a value, how should it typically be called?
option_b: Its result should be assigned, used in an expression, or printed
option_c: It cannot be called from main()
option_d: It must be called twice in a row
description: When a method returns a value, you usually capture or use that value: int larger = max(3, 4); or System.out.println(max(3, 4));. Otherwise the returned value is wasted.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: How is a method that returns void called?
option_a: It must be assigned to a variable
option_b: It must appear inside an expression
option_c: The call must be a stand-alone statement
description: A void method has no return value, so the call is just a statement: System.out.println("Hi");. You can't assign it to a variable.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is variable scope in Java?
option_a: The data type of a variable
option_b: The region of code in which a variable is accessible
option_c: The number of bytes a variable uses
option_d: Whether a variable is mutable or final
description: Scope defines the region of code where a variable is visible and accessible. In Java, variables are accessible only inside the region where they're declared.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: A variable declared directly inside a method is said to have which scope?
description: Method scope: a variable declared directly inside a method is available anywhere in that method after the line where it's declared.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: A variable declared inside a pair of curly braces { } (e.g., inside an if block) has which scope?
description: Block scope: variables declared inside { } (an if, for, etc.) are only accessible by code inside those braces.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: A variable declared outside all methods (but inside the class) has which scope?
description: Variables declared outside all methods (directly inside the class) have global scope — they're class fields accessible from any method of the class.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: Will this code compile? void foo() { if (true) { int y = 10; } System.out.println(y); }
option_b: No, y is out of scope outside the if block
option_d: Yes, but it throws a runtime exception
description: y has block scope — it's declared inside the if's braces and is undefined outside them. The compiler reports: cannot find symbol y.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: What is the output of: int sum = 0; for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { sum += i; } System.out.println(sum);
description: Sum of 1+2+3+4 = 10. The loop adds each i to sum while i <= 4.

lecture: Lecture 5: Loops & Methods (05-Development Software for Mobile.pdf)
question: How many times does this do-while body execute? int n = 100; do { System.out.println(n); n++; } while (n < 0);
description: Even though the condition n < 0 is false from the start, do-while always runs the body at least once. So it prints 100, then exits.
